data.title: Silver And Gold Intraday Outlook: The Metals Space Is As Confused As Traders Are
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Today is an unusual session for the markets following a chaotic day yesterday. Participants cannot be blamed for the current uncertainty; we are now in the aftermath of a series of decisions made by major central banks. Fundamentals are inconsistent, leading to a widespread sense of confusion across all asset classes, resulting in a slow consolidation.

Given that many of the world's most educated economists, like the Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell and other central bankers, seemingly struggle to understand the ongoing situation, it will be challenging to predict the market's direction once the uncertainty lifts.

Metals are not immune to this confusion, especially after experiencing volatile 10% swings up and down yesterday. They are today, however, experiencing a relatively calmer session: silver is up a shy 1%, gold retreats from $4,400 and other metals like platinum are hanging close to unchanged.

In general, after periods of significant volatility and a lull in news, the markets tend to trade within a range. This is precisely what we are witnessing today. The best approach now is to take a step back and prepare for what lies ahead. These types of sessions offer key breakout levels, as algos and traders await news to catalyze the next movement in the markets.

Let's explore the recent shifts in an intraday timeframe analysis of gold (XAU/USD) and silver (XAG/USD) to identify where the key levels to watch for breakouts are.

Gold (XAU/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Gold is now stuck in a triangle formation right at its quintessential $4,400 level – decisive for upcoming action. Remaining with +/- $75 of the psychological zone gives a further sense of uncertainty; hence, this provides key breakout levels to play as the week continues:

  • Above $4,475, bulls will hold the advantage until $4,700
  • Below $4,325, however, expect $4,100 to retest swiftly

Intraday timeframe levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • Short-term breakout level: $4,475
  • February wick pivot: $4,675-4,725
  • $4,850 to $4,900 key resistance
  • $5,100 pivotal resistance
  • $5,400 mini resistance

Support Levels:

  • Pivotal support: $4,325-4,400
  • Main channel lows support $4,100
  • Higher timeframe support: $3,880 to $4,000
  • $3,200 to $3,500 major support

Silver (XAG/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Silver has seen similar volatility and is now fighting to hold its $70 key level (+/- $1).

  • Rebounding and closing on a 4H candle above $71 would point to a swift retest of the $75 to $77.50 momentum pivot level, re-entering its 2025 bull channel
    • Extending above $80 puts back the trend in the bull's hand
  • Rejecting $69, however, would point to a retest of the $62 previous session support.
    • Breaking below $61 hints at the $50 to $55 October 2025 support

Higher timeframe levels to watch for silver (XAG/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $71 intraday resistance
  • Key momentum pivot: $74 to $77.50
  • Major resistance: $84.50 (50-Day MA)
  • Higher timeframe and war resistance: $90 to $95
  • Current record: $121.67

Support Levels:

  • December FOMC minor support: $64 to $66
  • $61.10 past session lows
  • $50 to $55 October resistance now major support
  • Silver's 2011 all-time highs: $49.81

Safe trades!

data.title: Richmond Manufacturing Index: Activity Was Flat In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Fifth district manufacturing activity was flat in March, according to the most recent survey from the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond. The composite manufacturing index rose ten points to 0, marking the first nonnegative reading in over a year. This month's reading was above the forecast of -8.

Here is an excerpt from the latest Richmond Fed manufacturing report:

All three of its component indexes rose in March. Shipments increased to −2 from −13, new orders improved to 4 from −9, and employment rose to −2 from −7.The local business conditions index increased to −5 in March from −15 in February. Meanwhile, the future local business conditions index decreased but remained in positive territory. The future indexes for shipments and new orders also decreased slightly but remained solidly in positive territory. The expectations index for employment rose to 14 from 6.The average growth rate of prices paid decreased somewhat, while average growth in prices received increased in March. Firms expected growth in both price measures to moderate over the next 12 months.

Background on Richmond Fed Manufacturing

The complete data series behind today's Richmond Fed manufacturing report, which dates from November 1993, is available here. The Richmond Manufacturing Index is a gauge of manufacturing activity in the Fifth Federal Reserve District (Maryland, North Carolina, the District of Columbia, Virginia, most of West Virginia, and South Carolina) compiled from a survey of ~100 manufacturers.

The composite manufacturing index is an average of indexes on shipments, new orders, order backlogs, capacity utilization, supplier lead times, number of employees, average workweek, wages, inventories, and capital expenditures.

This is a diffusion index, meaning negative readings indicate contraction and worsening conditions, while positive ones indicate expansion and improving conditions. The survey offers clues on inflationary pressures and the pace of growth in the manufacturing sector for this region of the country, and the accumulated results can help trace long-term trends.

Here is a snapshot of the complete Richmond Fed Manufacturing Composite series.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five.

data.title: Templeton Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equity markets collectively posted moderate gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a period of consolidation after the stronger advance seen in the third quarter.
  • Contributors: Stock selection and an overweight in the health care sector, stock selection in the utilities sector and stock selection and an underweight in the consumer discretionary sector contributed to relative performance for the quarter.
  • Detractors: Stock selection and overweights in the industrials, materials and communication services sectors detracted from relative performance for the quarter.
  • Outlook: We remain broadly constructive on the longer-term outlook for equity markets, but the strength of 2025's rally suggests that some caution is appropriate in the coming months.

Performance Review

  • Global equity markets collectively posted moderate gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a period of consolidation after the stronger advance seen in the third quarter. The fourth quarter unfolded against a backdrop of easing—but uneven—monetary policy expectations, greater stock-return dispersion across regions and sectors, and a rotation away from the most crowded growth investment themes. October experienced an extension of the post-summer stock rally as interest-rate cuts and generally resilient earnings supported risk appetite; November brought a pause as investors reassessed equity valuations and monetary policy timing; and December witnessed a stabilization of sentiment as central banks delivered broadly dovish outcomes while flagging increased data dependence into 2026.
  • The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Index-NR, for the quarter, driven by stock selection and an overweight in the health care sector, stock selection in the utilities sector and stock selection and an underweight in the consumer discretionary sector.
  • Eli Lilly's shares rose during the quarter as investors responded to continued strength in demand for its glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) obesity and diabetes treatments. The company reached a significant milestone during the quarter, becoming the first pharmaceutical firm to surpass a $1 trillion market capitalization on November 21. Sales of Mounjaro and Zepbound remained the key drivers of investor sentiment, supporting expectations for sustained growth across Lilly's metabolic health portfolio. That performance reinforces our investment thesis. Lilly's leadership in GLP-1 therapies positions the company to capture a large, structurally growing market, and we expect continued revenue and profit-margin expansion driven by Mounjaro and Zepbound, manufacturing scale and a deep R&D (research and development) pipeline. We view a premium valuation multiple as reasonable if Lilly delivers the 20%-plus revenue growth we expect over the next few years and converts pipeline optionality, including Orforglipron, into clinical and commercial progress. We continue to monitor the company's competition, regulatory and execution risks, but the combination of sustained commercial momentum and operational advantages supports our conviction in Lilly as a core growth holding.
  • Stock selection and overweights in the industrials, materials and communication services sectors detracted from relative performance for the quarter.
  • The shares of Universal Music Group (UMGNF) fell due to disappointing financial results. However, the company is revamping its streaming model to boost pricing control and profit margins, improve predictability and strengthen its market position. Better communication and potential US listing could add upside to the stock, and we view the company as having solid long-term growth prospects.
  • Regionally, stock selection and an underweight in the United States significantly contributed to relative performance for the quarter. Stock selection in the eurozone detracted from relative results.

Outlook

  • We remain broadly constructive on the longer-term outlook for equity markets, but the strength of 2025's rally suggests that some caution is appropriate in the coming months.
  • European equities' fourth-quarter outperformance reflected both structural sector advantages and a valuation buffer relative to US equities. Political and fiscal noise persisted, particularly in France, but the region's equity markets remained relatively insulated. Stocks were supported by a combination of defensive earnings profiles, sector composition, and improving policy clarity. Despite persistent skepticism, we believe the European economy looks poised for upside surprises in 2026.
  • In Japan, the recent change in government has reinforced the policy backdrop for reform and long-term growth. Japan has begun to lift interest rates for the first time in decades, which signals the return of inflation and a normalization of economic conditions after a prolonged deflationary period. This environment is particularly constructive for Japanese banks, which we believe could benefit from steeper yield curves and improving profitability. In our view, a gradual recovery in the yen, following an extended period of weakness, could also support domestic sectors.
  • Asia more broadly continues to benefit from a weaker US dollar and improving underlying momentum. We continue to find attractive bottom-up opportunities and expect to increase exposure as opportunities develop.

data.title: Columbia Select Short Corporate Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Select Short Corporate Income Fund Institutional Class shares returned 1.03% for the three months ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's primary benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Corporate Index, returned 1.26% for the same period.

Market overview

The fourth quarter in the United States was marked by significant economic data collection challenges stemming from the longest government shutdown in U.S. history. Running from October 1 to November 12, the 43-day shutdown surpassed the previous record of 35 days in 2018–2019, creating substantial complications for interpreting economic data and assessing the state of the U.S. economy.

The fourth quarter was characterized by relatively low volatility in U.S. financial markets, with machinations mostly tied to swings in sentiment related to the artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditure cycle. Returns were relatively muted. The S&P 500 Index rose 2.66% during the fourth quarter and 17.88% for the full year. The Nasdaq Composite gained 2.57% for the quarter and 20.17% for the year, while the Russell 2000 Index advanced 2.19% for the quarter and 12.81% for the year,

On the economic data front, the labor market showed continued cooling during the fourth quarter, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.6% in November (up from 4.4% in September), a four-year high. Nonfarm payrolls increased by just 64,000 in November, following a 105,000 decline in October, the largest decline since the end of 2020. The October payroll contraction was driven primarily by a 162,000 reduction in federal government employment, as workers who accepted the Trump administration's deferred resignation offers officially dropped off payrolls. Stripping out government job losses, private payroll growth remained in a relatively narrow range, recording 52,000 in October and 69,000 in November, with the unemployment rate increase attributable to those DOGE-related federal workforce reductions.

The Fed reduced the target range for the federal funds rate by 25 bps to 3.50%–3.75% at its December meeting, despite facing what Chairman Powell described as "dual mandate tension," with upside risks to inflation and downside risks to employment creating a challenging policy environment. The December decision was far from unanimous, with only nine of twelve members supporting the cut, including two official dissents and four soft dissents. Chair Powell acknowledged this unusual division, noting that persistent tension between the two parts of the mandate naturally produces such disagreement, with differences centered on how to weight competing risks, rather than disagreement about the underlying conditions. "Everyone around the table at the FOMC agrees that inflation is too high, and we want it to come down, and agrees that the labor market has softened and that there is further risk. Everyone agrees on that. Where the difference is, is how do you weight those risks...It's very unusual to have persistent tension between the two parts of the mandate. And when you do, this is what you see."

The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index (index) generated 0.84% of total return and -0.04% of excess returns (the impact of spread widening or tightening plus carry) in the fourth quarter. For the full year 2025, the index generated 7.77% total return and 1.19% of excess return.

Industrials, utilities and financials generated excess returns of -0.17%, 0.06% and 0.14%, respectively, in the fourth quarter. Mid-rated credits outperformed, as some idiosyncratic issues affected certain credits and sectors differently. For the fourth quarter, A rated corporates (0.04%) outperformed BBB rated corporates (0.07%), which outperformed AA rated corporates (-0.31%) and AAA rated corporates (-0.40%) on an excess return basis.

Outperforming industries over the quarter (from an excess return perspective) included transportation services, automotive, lodging, office REITS and airlines. The worst-performing industries over the quarter included cable satellite, media entertainment, technology, chemicals and wirelines.

From an issuance perspective, per Barclays index data, the fourth quarter of 2025 saw approximately $321 billion of U.S. dollar-denominated investment-grade corporate and noncorporate issuance, a 35% increase from the fourth quarter of 2024. The fourth quarter capped a year of robust new-issuance activity, with record monthly supply in both October and November pushing total 2025 issuance of $1.82 trillion almost 4% above 2024 levels. Total 2025 issuance was the second-highest issuance year, trailing only the 2020 COVID-impacted issuance record of $2.21 trillion.

Attribution

  • An overweight to credit risk, as measured by contribution-to-spread duration, marginally added to portfolio performance.
  • Security selection had a negative effect on performance over the quarter. The largest detraction came from an allocation to a technology company.
  • Industry allocation did not have a material effect on performance.
  • Duration, convexity and curve positioning detracted from overall performance.

Outlook

As we enter 2026, fundamentals for the investment-grade corporate market remain very solid. The Quarterly U.S. Investment-Grade Corporate Outlook for the fourth quarter of 2025, recently published by our Columbia Threadneedle Investments credit analyst partners, shows improvement in aggregate across various measures relative to the third-quarter estimates. The outlook for 2026 is also showing improvement. Expectations for revenue growth are slightly higher, EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) margins in aggregate are expected to expand slightly and capital expenditures were revised significantly higher, with a large step up in spending in the information technology sector and no changes to net leverage estimates — although investors are still expecting a slight decline year over year. However, spreads are starting 2026 at historically tight levels, with the widest spreads of the past six months representing the lowest six-month rolling maximum in history. This suggests very little upside potential from current levels.

Client portfolios enter 2026 in a similar position from a duration-times-spread (DTS) perspective, compared to where they started the fourth quarter — namely, essentially flat versus their respective benchmarks. Portfolios have ample liquidity to participate in what we expect to be a robust new-issuance calendar as we move into 2026 — issuance tied to AI-related capex and M&A-related financing. We believe the idiosyncratic nature of this issuance will lead to an increase in dispersion among issuers and sectors and could reshape credit curves with a bias to steepen, in our view, given expectations for a continuation/pickup in the percentage of longer-maturity issuance. We believe this type of market will favor our research-intensive, bottom-up security-selection investment process much more so than the market we have experienced of late, where macroeconomic factors primarily drove spread movements (beta) and there was a notable lack of dispersion (alpha).

data.title: Tracking Cliff Asness' AQR Capital Management 13F Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to AQR Capital Management’s US stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their 13F Form filed on 02/17/2026. Please check out our last update for the moves during Q3 2025.

This quarter, AQR Capital Management’s 13F portfolio value increased from ~$156B to ~$191B. The portfolio typically has over 2000 positions. Only 28 stakes are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio), and they are the focus of this article. The five largest stakes are Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon.com. Together, they account for ~9% of the total portfolio value.

Note: Cliff Asness co-founded AQR Capital Management in 1998. AQR Capital Management is one of the largest Quant firms with over $150B of Assets Under Management. They combine different quantitative styles (value, momentum, etc.) to construct diversified risk-adjusted portfolios that have a low correlation to equity-only portfolios. They have several mutual funds with different styles, with the multi-asset fund (AQRIX) as the flagship. The varied offerings limit the utility of quarter-over-quarter 13F comparisons for most of their reported positions. However, analyzing the top positions can offer valuable insights into their broader strategy.

Stake Increases:

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA): NVDA is currently the largest 13F position at 2.57% of the portfolio. It was first purchased in 2006 at prices under a dollar, after accounting for stock splits. Recent activity follows. There was a ~150% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$27 and ~$47. The next two quarters saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$41 and ~$50. That was followed by a ~18% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$104 and ~$153. The last quarter saw another ~14% stake increase at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. This quarter also saw a ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$170 and ~$212. The stock currently trades at ~$175.

Apple Inc. (AAPL): AAPL is now the second largest 13F position in the portfolio at 1.78%. It is a very long-term stake that has been in the portfolio since 2003. Recent activity follows. There was a ~27% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$221 and ~$259. That was followed by a ~16% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$244 and ~$289. The stock currently trades at ~$254.

Microsoft Corp (MSFT): MSFT is a large (top three) 1.59% of the portfolio position first purchased during 2004 in the high-20s price range. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. H2 2024 saw a ~8% stake increase at prices between ~$395 and ~$468. That was followed by a similar increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The last quarter saw another ~37% stake increase at prices between ~$489 and ~$556. The stock currently trades at ~$374. There was a marginal increase this quarter.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): GOOGL is a 1.49% long-term stake in the portfolio first purchased during 2005. The stake has wavered. Recent activity follows. Q3 2024 saw a ~15% selling at prices between ~$149 and ~$191, and that was followed by a ~11% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$162 and ~$197. There was a ~15% stake increase during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$151 and ~$207. The last quarter saw another ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$173 and ~$256. That was followed by another ~13% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$236 and ~$329. The stock currently trades at ~$294.

Amazon.com (AMZN): The 1.08% stake in AMZN was first purchased during 2004. It was a minutely small stake at the time. The next several years saw incremental buying but the position remained very small. 2013 saw a large purchase in the mid-teens price range. Recent activity follows. data.title: Nomura Mid Cap Income Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

US equities rose in 4Q25 amid a complex mix of optimism about growth driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and growing concerns about profitability, spending, and macroeconomic uncertainty. While enthusiasm for AI investments remained a defining theme, investor scrutiny increased as questions emerged regarding return on investment, balance sheet leverage, and the sustainability of elevated capital expenditures.

Macroeconomic conditions showed signs of moderation. Labor markets weakened, with the unemployment rate rising to a four-year high in November, alongside a wave of high-profile layoffs across technology and consumer-oriented companies. Consumer behavior remained uneven: while retail sales exceeded expectations, supporting resilience narratives, Visa (V) reported that growth in holiday spending was driven primarily by ecommerce and electronics.

The Russell Midcap® Index rose 0.16% for the quarter, with healthcare, materials, and information technology (IT) among the strongest contributing sectors, while real estate, communication services, and utilities posted the weakest results.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise indicated.

Within the Fund

In 4Q25, Nomura Mid Cap Income Opportunities Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell Midcap Index. Dividend income was a positive for the Fund but was not enough to overcome underperformance from stock selection compared with the benchmark during the period.

Negative stock selection in industrials was the primary detractor during the quarter with adverse stock selection in consumer discretionary and materials also detracting. While sector allocation was a positive factor, it was not sufficient to overcome negative stock selection, which was the main detractor from performance. Within sector allocation, the Fund's significant overweight of the materials sector contributed but not enough to overcome adverse stock selection in the sector. Underweight allocations in utilities and communication services also proved beneficial during the period.

Within industrials, distributors including Fastenal Co. (FAST) and Wasco Inc. were both detractors from performance as the rate of change in their businesses slowed. Garmin Ltd. (GRMN), a leading provider of global positioning system technology for various consumer markets, delivered solid growth but fell short of high investor expectations in its outdoor products segment.

Healthcare was the largest contributor to performance during the period. Cardinal Health Inc. (CAH), a leading distributor of pharmaceuticals and medical products, experienced a lift in growth from specialty pharmaceutical markets. Within IT, Seagate Technology Holdings PLC (STX), a multinational provider of hard-disk-drive technologies, was a top contributor for the second quarter in a row. In consumer discretionary, Travel + Leisure Co. (TNL), a leader in membership and leisure travel, benefited from a resurgence in membership vacation demand.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we expect markets to be influenced by the ongoing tug of war between secular growth opportunities and ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty. While AI investment continues to reshape capital spending priorities across the economy, we believe investor focus will increasingly shift toward profitability, free cash flow generation, and disciplined capital allocation.

We expect earnings dispersion to remain wide, favoring companies with durable competitive advantages, strong balance sheets, and the ability to compound earnings across varying economic conditions. Periods of volatility may continue to create attractive entry points, and we remain disciplined in balancing growth opportunities with valuation and risk considerations as we position the portfolio for the year ahead.

data.title: Agree Realty Won't Disappoint You, But It's Also Not A Buy
data.description:
I like Agree Realty (ADC). I always did. I often see people comparing it to Realty Income (O). Why? Because of its monthly dividends, triple net lease model, and similar segment focus. But there's more to ADC in my view, and I will discuss that in this article.

A few of my latest articles about ADC were a hold. I see that people sometimes read this as me being bearish on ADC. But that's not the case.

When I rate ADC a hold, I want to show you that I have enough faith in the business to keep owning it. I have some shares in ADC that I was lucky to buy at a lower price. How much lower? My cost average is in the $50s.

I see that's changed now. I saw ADC's stock price was even above $80 a few weeks back. Yes, I like the business very much, and I'm happy that the valuation is growing. But I don't like to sugarcoat things, so I will state right from the get-go: I'm not buying ADC at these levels.

But I'm not selling shares either. Where does that leave us? ADC remains a hold for me. I decided to prepare a follow-up coverage as ADC published its annual financials. And I want to give you my outlook on its valuation as a lot has happened in the last 6 months.

Yes, the last time I covered ADC was in September 2025, so I think the follow-up is a good idea, especially as I know many of my readers like Agree Realty very much.

The Market Is Scared Of The Inflation Risk

A table I pasted below shows PPI, which is a producer price index. As you can see, the market expected a 2.9% metric. But it surprised the market and me, not in a good way, and amounted to 3.4%

I'm also pasting the comment by U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics shared by Trading Economics:

US producer prices climbed 3.4% year-over-year in February 2026, marking the largest increase in a year and exceeding market expectations of 2.9%. Core producer prices, excluding food and energy, also jumped 3.9% year-over-year, the biggest rise in three years and surpassing market forecasts of 3.7%.

Some of you may think: "Yes, there's a war in the Middle East, so that has to be the reason for it." But it's not that easy, because conflict started at the end of February 2026. So the PPI increase wasn't caused by that.

I realize the market didn't like that information. Especially in the REIT field. It doesn't surprise me at all, as I think REITs perform best in a relatively low interest rate environment. Result? Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund (VNQ) dropped 2.8%, and the likes of ADC or O followed with even higher decreases.

Is the risk of inflation back on the table? If so, the Fed may be forced to slow down with cutting rates. That is not good for REITs. I know that lower interest rates are (or were) one of the catalysts that should help them perform better. But that may be harder given the conflict in the Middle East and the inflation data I showed you.

But Agree Realty Is Different

At Q4 2025, ADC owned 2,674 properties, and in my taste, it just has one of the best portfolios in the entire sector. From several perspectives. What I like very much is a high share of IG tenants (nearly 67%) and the fact that Walmart (which I consider a very solid tenant) is the #1 tenant with a 5.6% share in ABR. I'm pasting the structure below.

Need a comparison to ADC's peers? Look below. That's IG share in ABR and sector concentration. Based on that and a better occupancy (up to 99.7%), I feel comfortable to state: I consider ADC's portfolio as one of the best in the sector.

I like to remind readers that REITs grow in two main ways. Buying more properties and increasing rent. Of course, there's a limit to how fast ADC can grow its rent. I usually see rent escalators average to 1% to 2%. That's not enough to satisfy investors, isn't it? That's why investing matters.

In 2025 ADC realized one of its best-ever investment volumes (over $1.5B), which I was very happy about. Any expectations for 2026? Yes, and they look good to me too, as they range from $1.4B to $1.6B.

Make no mistake, ADC's growth was top-tier, one of the best in this field. Here's the management's take on that from the last Earnings Call:

In a dynamic macro environment, we remain disciplined, continued investing in our future and delivered over 4.5% AFFO per share growth. The $1.55 billion invested across our 3 investment platforms was the second highest total in company history representing more than 60% year-over-year growth. As demonstrated by our 2026 guidance, the fundamentals supporting our outlook are very strong. Our portfolio has never been better positioned, the depth and strength of our team is exceptional, and our balance sheet is in tremendous shape.

How's the financing structure? I see a D/EV of 27.4% and no material maturities until 2028. ADC also showcased a fixed charge coverage of 4.2x and an A- rating from Fitch. That gives me no worries about ADC's business or its ability to deal with debt.

I consider its dividends safe, especially with a 70% forward AFFO payout ratio. I'm looking at a history of nearly 170 consecutive payments. What was their CAGR? North of 5% during the 10-year period. You know me, I'm not sugar-coating, so I will state the obvious: The DPS growth has slowed down during the last couple of years. But let's keep in mind that REITs didn't have it easy since 2022. In the end, I'm confident in ADC's payments, and I wish to collect more.

ADC Is Not An Opportunity Anymore

Is there a problem with ADC?. Yes, its valuation. I see ADC trades at almost 17x forward P/FFO ratio. I don't have to look hard for comparisons; Alpine Income (PINE) did that for me by including the chart below in its last investor presentation.

What REIT is at the top of the chart? ADC. By a lot. Comparing ADC to O, for instance, I see a 310 bps gap. Yes, I realize that ADC is much smaller and has better growth prospects. But I consider buying ADC at these prices as paying too high of a premium.

I think the business is great and the balance sheet is healthy, and I can't complain about ADC's investment decisions. But business is one side of the story. Valuation is the other. With ADC, I'm a huge fan of the business. Not so much of a valuation.

That's why ADC continues to be a hold for me. And don't get me wrong, I'm not selling any of my shares. But I'm not buying either.

In my opinion, ADC needs one of two things to happen before I rate it a buy again: 1. a meaningful sell-off or 2. time. Time for ADC to grow its AFFO per share, to grow into its valuation. I would be happy to invest more at a lower multiple. But for now, ADC is a hold for me.

data.title: DHT: BW Overhang Almost Gone, Q2 Dividend Could Top 20%
data.description:
Investment Thesis

DHT Holdings (DHT) is experiencing astonishing rates despite the stock not reflecting them. I expect that Q2 yield could be above 20% annualized, depending on achieved rates. Moreover, if the Strait of Hormuz reopens in the next few weeks, rates could remain elevated during all of 2026.

Background

DHT is a crude oil shipping company and the only listed company fully focused on the VLCC segment.

Its fleet consists of 23 on-the-water vessels plus two new builds that will be delivered in the next few months. On a fully delivered basis, the fleet will remain at 23 vessels since the company agreed to sell its two 2007 VLCC, its oldest vessels for more than $50 million each. Despite the four new builds (two already delivered), the fleet is relatively old, with an average age of almost 9 years. This is because all vessels, excluding the four 2026 ones, were built between 2011 and 2018. To compensate for the older fleet, all its VLCC are scrubber fitted. On the Q4 conference call, management commented that they don’t plan to sell any more vessels.

So we are done selling for now. So these -- we have 5 ships that are built in 2011, 2012. They're fantastic ships, large deadweight, excellent fuel economics, very, very good condition, and they serviced both us and our customers very well, and they are earning top dollars in the market. So they're not going anywhere but staying in the DHT fleet.

Another characteristic of the company is that it typically has several vessels chartered out, limiting its spot exposure. Given the uptick in rates at the start of the year, the company took the opportunity and chartered out three ships at astonishing rates between $90,000 per day and $105,000. Right now, the company has 12 vessels chartered out, which is around 50% of the fleet, but two of them will finish their current contract at the end of the quarter.

This charter out strategy provides some earnings stability that the company uses to pay generous dividends. In 2022 the company announced its new dividend policy to pay 100% of net income as a quarterly dividend.

Finally, another important topic is its shareholder base. Until recently its main shareholder was BW Group Limited, which obtained 33.5% of DHT shares as a payment for its 11 VLCCs in 2017.

During the years BW Group has been reducing its participation, but this has accelerated in the last few months. In November 2025 the group still owned more than 10% of DHT, but this March, BW Group communicated that they already owned less than 5%. I don’t know if they will sell all their stake or will stop for now, but either way a huge overhang for the stock will finish shortly as the selling pressure will disappear.

Financial Position and Valuation

DHT has a rock-solid balance sheet, with $79 million in cash and just $429 million in debt at the end of Q4 2025. However, with the proceeds from the last vessel sales, cash will increase by more than $100 million. With the new build deliveries, debt will increase slightly but will remain at low levels.

Q4 results were in line with estimates, with an EPS of $0.41 and the same dividend. However, Q1 guidance was a bit underwhelming, with 76% of spot rates fixed at just $78,900 per day. For reference, the Q4 fleet average rate was $60,300 per day, with spot rates at $69,500 and chartered out at $49,400 per day.

With that guidance and the subsequent rates evolution, I expect at least $0.6 EPS in Q1 and a similar dividend, representing almost a 15% yield.

But Q2 will be much better; DHT had some stellar rates:

DHT Puma (the first one in the image) is chartered out with profit sharing, implying that DHT will not benefit from it; however, DHT Mustang (the bottom one) is in the spot market and fixed a stellar rate. I believe that the VLCC was expected to load in the AG, meaning that they may need to renegotiate the fixture; however, I still expect an outstanding rate. Also, it is important to note that DHT does not have any vessel trapped inside the Strait of Hormuz.

Assuming lower average Q2 spot rates, around $150,000 per day, suggests that Q2 EPS will be around $0.9. If they pay that as a dividend, it gives a yield above 22%.

Regarding valuation, I estimate DHT NAV around $15.5, meaning the company is trading at 1.1x NAV. VLCC players and companies paying a substantial portion of dividends tend to trade at a premium. Assuming a 1.3x NAV, DHT fair value should be around $20, representing a 20% upside.

However, if rates remain elevated, I expect the company to start trading on yield at even more elevated levels.

Risks

The main risk is if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for many months. In that scenario, there would be an oversupply of vessels depressing rates. However, transits are increasing (under Iran control) and it seems there will be agreements with some countries. If those countries use legit fleet tankers, as one would expect from Japan, this will be positive for the market.

Another risk is a global recession and a market crash. Elevated oil prices will reduce crude demand and could trigger a global recession. That will negatively impact the stock. Moreover, it wouldn’t surprise me to see a sharp correction in the stock market. If global market valuations decrease substantially, it will impact DHT stock price even if rates remain elevated.

Conclusion

DHT is printing cash with current rates while the stock is losing ground. While I understand some fear due to the uncertainty around the Strait of Hormuz situation, for now rates have remained strong.

Moreover, DHT has secured great charters for some of its fleet, ensuring profitability even if rates come down significantly.

I expect rates to come down a bit, but if the Strait of Hormuz reopens in a month, there will be a huge dislocation of vessels, and rates will move up even further, ensuring a great 2026. Even if it remains partially closed to Western vessels, if Asian buyers are allowed to transit it, I expect this to be positive for all tankers.

While I don’t have a position right now, I plan to buy if the stock reaches NAV or if there are positive signs that transits through the Strait of Hormuz continue to increase.

data.title: The 'Problem' With Micron's Guidance
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The market reaction to Micron Technology, Inc.’s (MU) stellar Q2 FY26 ER might have perplexed just about every investor.

After all, the company beat the midpoint of consensus expectations by an eye-popping 24%, implying Q2 FY26 revenue y/y growth of 196% or a ~3x growth pace. That is the strongest y/y revenue growth rate Micron has ever delivered since 1998.

Markets should have done a victory lap with Micron’s shares after the fiscal Q2 results. Except markets sold the stock off, and Micron lost ~$64B in market cap after the results were announced.

The problem lies in the details of Micron’s forward-looking plans—its capex raise to $25B and outlook on spending for FY27.

However, I believe investors are ignoring the details in Micron’s guidance, disregarding the generational tailwinds that Micron is benefiting from and focusing too much on the capex spending "problem," which is, in reality, growth-accretive for Micron.

I explain why investors should ignore the market's fears about Micron’s capex plans and stay bullish on its shares.

Micron Is Benefiting From Unprecedented Tailwinds

My last coverage note on Micron focused on its NAND business and the SSD tailwinds. The strength in Micron’s NAND business was particularly evident in Q2, with NAND revenues more than doubling sequentially, growing faster than the DRAM business on a q/q basis.

Management is seeing “NAND demand significantly in excess of” the company’s supply, implying elevated supply constraints in the NAND business. At GTC26, Nvidia (NVDA) signaled strong usage of KV cache management for optimizing inference, which should exacerbate demand for SSDs (which are all NAND-based), and Micron’s management addressed the point on the Q2 FY26 call.

It’s not just NAND. Micron’s breadwinning memory product so far, high bandwidth memory aka HBM (which is DRAM-based), continues to also be facing its own supply constraint issues as demand significantly outpaces supply. Micron has already begun volume shipping of its HBM4 36GB 12H product and is currently engaging customers like Nvidia for its HBM4 48GB 16H products, which should begin ramping in CY27.

Demand is so strong for Micron’s memory and storage products that management as well as sell-side analysts are chasing their own tails in hiking forward revenue forecasts. Take a look at the chart below, where consensus estimates for Micron’s forward revenues now imply 190% growth in FY26, 87 massive points higher than what sell-side was expecting before last week.

(Nvidia’s GTC26 and Micron’s Q2 FY26 ER were last week.)

Based on Trendforce data, DRAM prices (DDR5 16Gb 2Gx8 4800/5600) are already up ~12% YTD and ~603% y/y, while NAND prices (512Gb TLC) are up ~51% YTD and ~730%.

These are unprecedented surges in DRAM/NAND prices, and Micron’s management has decided to make the best of such generational opportunities by hiking its capex plans. On the Q2 FY26 call, Micron told investors that they plan to raise FY26 capex to ~$25B, up 39% from $18B last quarter.

While the 39% sequential increase in capex plans may have spooked some investors, it’s also likely that Micron’s $10B spending bill item for FY27 could have caused some more investor concern.

The Confusion Around Micron’s Capex Guidance

Before I move ahead, I will address a small nuance regarding Micron’s capex.

Typically there are 3 components of Micron’s capex bills—WFE (wafer equipment) spending, construction spending (on new greenfield/cleanroom), and test/assembly spending.

The general consensus among investors is usually that when Micron hikes capex guidance, “especially when capex dollars are allocated for technology transitions,” as I stated here, markets turn bullish on Micron’s shares because the view is Micron can generate a quicker ROI on capex investments that are allocated towards tech transitions—newer equipment, more leading-edge nodes, etc.

However, when Micron allocates capex towards construction of greenfield/cleanroom locations, the perceived view is that the ROI on construction-related dollars will take longer. There are two recent time periods that come to mind where Micron stated that they would allocate relatively higher capex towards construction vs. WFE spending, i.e., the 2019-2020 and 2022-2023 time periods. During these 2 periods, Micron’s capital efficiency metrics, such as ROIC and ROA, took a hit, as I have depicted below.

Since management revealed on the Q2 FY26 call that they plan to spend ~$10B on construction of new projects in FY27, markets are likely feeling a sense of déjà vu given how ROIC and ROA took a hit during past cycles of elevated construction capex for Micron. In all likelihood, markets are viewing Micron’s capex guidance as a “problem” that imposes on the company’s capital efficiency and asset utilization.

The irony that I find during the current bout of consternation that the market is feeling is that markets are ignoring how Micron got here—with DRAM/NAND prices skyrocketing at least 5-6x y/y and with the outlook on prices continuing to stay strong as per industry reports, as noted in my previous month's coverage on Micron.

As markets digest through Micron’s report, I believe markets are not pricing in the future capacity that Micron is already planning to build today. The weakness in Micron’s shares is a classic mispricing of Micron's outlook without understanding the depth in Micron’s capex guidance and the growth ramps that management is already building based on the price outlook for DRAM/NAND.

I have stated in many of my prior Micron coverages that the price outlook for DRAM/NAND are the two best indicators for staying bullish on Micron versus valuation metrics. SA’s valuation metric dashboard for Micron shows the company’s shares are undervalued on many metrics, but I continue to stay bullish because I expect higher DRAM prices to support the bullish outlook for Micron and its ~20% or higher bit shipment outlook.

Risks To Thesis & Other Factors To Note

As I mentioned earlier, a top in DRAM/NAND prices, or worse, a contraction in prices, will severely impact Micron’s outlook.

Additionally, on a broader level, if the overall AI capex growth slows, Micron will also get affected.

At this point allow me to put forth a very bullish observation for investors. We are 7 days away from closing March, and there have been zero insiders selling Micron shares. This is after relatively lower insider selling seen last month despite Micron’s shares growing ~4x since September last year.

That does look very bullish to me.

Takeaway

I believe investors are reading too much in the tape regarding Micron’s capex guidance. I understand the sense of déjà vu investors may be feeling given how capital efficiency metrics get hurt during periods of elevated construction capex, but then, Micron is also benefiting from the generational opportunity of a lifetime with an unprecedented surge in DRAM and NAND prices.

I am reiterating my bullishness on Micron’s shares.

data.title: Time To Short The S&P 500
data.description:
My 2026 Market Outlook article – titled “The Market Outlook for 2026 Looks Grim – Buckle Up!” – was published on December 29, 2025. In that article, I issued a ‘sell’ rating for the S&P 500. As a part of the research for this article, I went through and counted the various market outlook articles on the 2026 Market Outlook page to see what the relative distribution of various ratings were. By my count, the breakdown was as follows:

As you can see, my ‘sell’ rating on the S&P 500 was very much not the popular position. Nevertheless, this has been the performance of the S&P 500 so far this year:

In this article, I’m going to make the argument that the bad news is potentially just getting started for S&P 500 investors this year. While I have gestured towards the idea that some investors might even want to short the S&P 500 in previous articles that have touched on my bearishness in relation to that index, I have never given that action what I would deem as a full-throated endorsement. In this article, by contrast, I will argue that now is definitely looking like the time to short the S&P 500 if there ever was one.

So, let’s get into the reasons for this high-level of bearish conviction.

The US-Israel-Iran Conflict is not “Liberation Day”

I’ve heard market commentators mention supposed parallels between the ongoing US-Israel-Iran conflict and the “Liberation Day” market sell-off from April of 2025. The relevant supposed analogous idea was that, after sufficient pain in markets, Trump inevitably pulls back under the pressure so as not to send various markets into a state of irreparable damage. People say it was the bond market that forced Trump’s hand in April of last year. They are saying that it’s the oil markets that are forcing Trump’s recent pull-back vis-à-vis Iran.

This analogizing of the two periods suggests that Trump has the power to undo damage this time around in a way similar to how his pull-back last April sent markets into a bull run of ~40% from the lows to the peak in January of 2026. It’s not so clear that this is the case, however. When Trump came out on April 9th of last year and talked about his plans to pull-back on his previous tariff plans, markets rallied in a violent way to the upside, gaining ~9.5% on the day of the announcement alone:

The magnitude of the one-day move here, which can be seen on the above chart, speaks to the power that Trump had then. What he said massively moved markets on that day.

By contrast, with the US-Israel-Iran conflict, it’s not so clear Trump has the same sort of power to fix things. Even though markets had a nice relief rally on Monday morning when Trump’s plans to pull back on attacks and seek more of a diplomatic resolution came out, that rally wasn’t anything like the rally post Liberation Day last year. As with before, we can see this in looking at the chart:

This 1.15% move on the day was just a normal-looking relief rally against the back-drop of a market that is noticeably trending downward, not some massive reversal signaling reasons for significant bullish sentiment to come.

If the current situation really is not fully within Trump’s control anymore, it’s not clear that the bulls can lean on the “Trump put” to protect against the downside here.

Damage to Oil Markets is Much Deeper Than Tariffs

And this should not be surprising when one reflects on what’s been going on within energy markets in the wake of the US-Isreal-Iran conflict. This is not putting on tariffs that you can unilaterally rescind without much damage done as was the case last year. This is perhaps the worst case of the war-in-the-middle-east type of scenarios that energy and geopolitical experts have been worried about for years. After the missiles have started flying, vital infrastructure has been destroyed, and reasons for vengeance between sworn enemies have been inflamed, it’s really hard to put that sort of genie back in the bottle. Some fear the effects on energy markets could last for a long time, even if the war were to end immediately. So, while overtures of preferences for diplomacy are good, it really seems unclear as to when markets really can be expecting to get out from under the economic headwinds the hostilities have created.

Furthermore, while many people look at the current state of energy markets and immediately begin to get worried about a future ramp up in inflation, history suggests that perhaps it could be worse and that we should be thinking in the other direction.

Rather than being inflationary, historically, as the above chart suggests, oil price shocks seem to have a disinflationary/deflationary ultimate effect, leading to recessionary periods as wide swathes of consumer spending get redirected to higher energy prices.

Thus, if this thing has really gotten outside of Trump’s ability to control (as the market’s relatively tepid reaction to his announcement of looking for a diplomatic off-ramp suggests), and its ultimate effects could substantially increase the chances for recession, the outlook for the S&P 500 and markets generally would seem to be on very shaky footing.

A Multitude of Additional Headwinds

And while the US-Israel-Iran conflict, and perhaps Trump’s perceived inability to fully put that genie back in the bottle there, is the most proximate reason for heightened bearishness in the general investor zeitgeist, there are also a multitude of other important risk factors to take heed of.

There’s the situation in private credit (which I’ve written about here and here) which has ominous rhyming overtones with the 2007-08 period. There’s the AI bubble which seems doomed to have negative effects whether AI succeeds or fails to live up to expectations. There’s the labor market which is showing deteriorating signs with each new report that we’re seeing of late, along with persistent inflation that has stubbornly refused to come under the Fed’s control for years now. Finally, there's the fact that we sit at high to extremely high levels of valuation relative to history, no matter what metric you choose to look at - e.g., P/E ratio of the S&P 500 on a TTM or FWD basis, the Shiller P/E ratio, the Buffett Indicator, etc. All these factors on their own could quite possibly be enough to justify a sustained downward move in markets. Taken together, they seemingly take us about as close to a level of certainty as one can have in investing that such future downside really is on the horizon.

Breaching the 200-day Moving Average

And all this bad news and reason for dread seems to be being priced into markets of late with the S&P 500 having experienced a sustained downtrend the last several weeks, culminating in a breach of the all-important 200-day moving average.

As Paul Tudor Jones famously said, “nothing good happens below the 200-day moving average.” And, against the back-drop of the current conditions we find ourselves in, I don’t think it’s the right time to test that particular piece of wisdom.

Shorting the S&P 500

When discussion of shorting the S&P 500 comes up, the first thing to notice is the comparative level of risk involved here. In general, investors want to be long the S&P 500, given its track record of success and the track record of the U.S. economy. Taking a bearish view on the S&P 500 can be particularly risky, given this backdrop on the long side. Nevertheless, I think there are times when it can pay to have a bearish view. And I think right now is as about as obvious a case of one of those times as an investor could ask for.

So, how to do it then? Well, there’s always the option of literally shorting an S&P 500 ETF or the like. However, this would definitely not be my preferred method. Additionally, there’s the option of using put options. However, this is not something within everyone’s wheelhouse for one reason or another.

To my mind, a relatively convenient way to short the S&P 500 is to use an inverse ETF like Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bear 1X ETF (SPDN) or ProShares Short S&P500 ETF (SH). While these ETFs only claim to attempt to match 1-day moves inverse to the performance of the S&P 500, the aggregate performance over longer periods has historically tracked the inverse of the S&P 500 pretty decently. For instance, here is the performance over the last 6 months for SPDN:

Here is the performance over the last 6 months for SH:

And because these funds don’t use leverage, they don’t run into the kind of degree of volatility decay issues that can be characteristic of leveraged ETF products, a feature of leveraged ETFs that is so often pointed to as a reason against using a longer-than-one-day holding period.

Additionally, the expense ratios are reasonable on these products – 0.89% for SH, 0.48% for SPDN. In my opinion, these are reasonable prices to pay as an alternative to having to short the S&P 500 through ETFs oneself or go through the process of buying puts to take a short position.

The liquidity on these products is also quite attractive. Per cnbc.com, the average 10-day volume for SH was 17.5 million shares (about $664 million in notional value at current prices), whereas for SPDN it was 122.74 million shares (about $1.2 billion in notional value at current prices).

Finally, there’s the added perk that these ETFs pay dividends which can offer a little downside cushion. On a prior twelve-month basis, the yield was 4.2% for SH, whereas for SPDN it was 3.8%, based on current market prices.

All in all, my preference would be for SPDN, given the relatively higher level of liquidity and lower expense ratio. However, ProShares is a big brand-name in this space, and I don’t think you could go wrong with SH for the sort of purposes I’m talking about here.

Risk to Thesis and Investor Takeaway

As mentioned already, it’s important to understand that shorting the S&P 500 is inherently riskier than being long the S&P 500, generally speaking. A number of the reasons for bearishness that I cite might be off in some way as to the magnitude of the importance I think they have or that they will even come to pass in the first place in a meaningful way. Additionally, shorting requires a lot better timing than is usually required in going long the index. As such, I would recommend using stop-losses and having some sort of pre-defined level of loss set before going into the trade and making sure to stick to it.

The thing is, though, that if we are going into a period of more downside pain to come, as I expect we are, there oftentimes can be very few ways to make positive returns. Instead, the idea often becomes focusing on capital preservation. Shorting the S&P 500 strikes me as a particularly obvious way at the moment, though, to have some meaningful prospect for upside against the backdrop of a period which I suspect is otherwise going to be pretty painful for investors.
